--- Page 1 ---
ON THE MUTUAL VISIBILITY OF SOME MOORE GRAPHS
TONNY K B AND SHIKHI M
Abstract. The concept of mutual visibility in a graph encodes combinatorial information
about vertex subsets with prescribed visibility properties and serves as a useful algebraic in-
variant. In this paper, we study the visibility properties of some Moore graphs of diameter 2,
with emphasis on the Petersen and Hoffman–Singleton graphs. We first compute the visibility
polynomial of the Petersen graph explicitly by analyzing its structural features in relation to the
strong regularity of the graph. For the Hoffman–Singleton graph, we establish an upper bound
of 20 on its visibility number, and subsequently employ an integer programming approach to
prove that this bound is tight. As a corollary, we deduce that the maximum size of an in-
duced matching in the Hoffman–Singleton graph is 10. These results demonstrate how visibility
polynomials can be computed for extremal graphs of high symmetry, and how algebraic and
optimization techniques can be combined to determine precise visibility parameters in complex
graph families.
1. Introduction
Let G(V, E) be a simple graph and let X ⊆V . Two vertices u, v ∈V are said to be X-visible
[27] if there exists a shortest path P from u to v such that the internal vertices of P do not
belong to X; that is, V (P) ∩X ⊆{u, v}. A set X is called a mutual-visibility set of G if every
pair of vertices in X is X-visible. The maximum size of such a set in G is referred to as the
mutual-visibility number, denoted by µ(G).
The notion of mutual-visibility in graphs has received increasing attention because of its sig-
nificance across both theoretical and practical domains. Wu and Rosenfeld first explored the
foundational visibility problems in the context of pebble graphs [24, 31]. Later, Di Stefano intro-
duced the formal definition of mutual-visibility sets in graph-theoretic terms [27]. The concept
of mutual-visibility serves as a powerful tool for analyzing the transmission of information, in-
fluence, or coordination under topological constraints. This graph-theoretic framework has been
the subject of numerous investigations [4–6, 13, 14, 20–22, 28], and several variants of mutual
visibility have also been proposed in [12].
An associated notion is that of a general position set, which was independently proposed in
[23, 30]. This concept describes a subset of vertices in a graph where no three distinct vertices
lie along the same shortest path. Formally, a set S ⊆V (G) in a connected graph G becomes
a general position set if none of its vertices appear on a geodesic between any two others in
the set. The idea has been extended by defining the general position polynomial of a graph, as
introduced and examined in [19].
In practical applications, mutual visibility is significant in robotics. In multi-agent systems,
robots must often reposition themselves to ensure unobstructed visibility between all agents,
facilitating decentralized control algorithms for formation, navigation, and surveillance in un-
known or dynamic environments. For detailed studies, see [1–3, 10, 11, 16, 25].
In robotic systems and sensor networks, effective coordination, coverage, and communication
often rely on the concept of visibility between agents or nodes in the system.
Studying mutual visibility sets of all orders enables a deeper understanding of how groups
of agents can observe each other simultaneously, which is critical for tasks such as surveillance,
target tracking, and distributed decision-making. In environments with varying visibility con-
straints due to obstacles, analyzing mutual-visibility sets of all sizes helps to determine how
many agents can simultaneously maintain line-of-sight and adjust their positions accordingly.
2010 Mathematics Subject Classification. 05C30, 05C31, 05C76.
Key words and phrases. mutual-visibility set, visibility polynomial.
1
arXiv:2510.25858v1  [math.CO]  29 Oct 2025


--- Page 2 ---
2
TONNY K B AND SHIKHI M
In an important development in this direction, B. Csilla et.al. introduced the visibility polyno-
mial in [7], which serves as a polynomial invariant capturing the distribution of mutual-visibility
sets of all orders within a graph. Let G be a graph of order n. The visibility polynomial of G,
denoted by V(G), is defined in [7] as
V(G) =
X
i≥0
rixi,
where ri denotes the number of mutual-visibility sets of G having cardinality i. The visibility
polynomial encodes the number of mutual-visibility sets of all orders within a graph, and the
process of enumerating these sets provides a deeper understanding of their structural properties.
In [29], the present authors investigated the visibility polynomial associated with the join of two
graphs.
This paper examines the visibility properties of certain Moore graphs, with a focus on the
Peterson graph and the Hoffman-Singleton graph. The visibility polynomial of the Peterson
graph is explicitly stated. In the paper, algebraic and optimization techniques are combined to
prove that the visibility number of the Hoffman-Singleton graph is 20. This result leads to the
conclusion that the maximum size of an induced matching in the Hoffman-Singleton graph is
ten.
2. Notations and preliminaries
In this paper, G(V, E) represents an undirected simple connected graph with vertex set V
and edge set E. For standard graph-theoretic definitions and notations presented in the paper,
refer [17].
The union of two graphs G1(V1, E1) and G2(V2, E2) is denoted by G1 ∪G2 having vertex set
V1 ∪V2 and edge set E1 ∪E2. The union of graphs having no vertices in common and no edges
in common is referred to as the disjoint union of the graphs. The disjoint union of n copies of
a graph G is denoted by nG. G denotes the complement of a graph G which has the same set
of vertices as that of G and two vertices in G are adjacent if and only if they are not adjacent
in G. A complete graph Kn on n vertices is a graph in which every pair of distinct vertices is
connected by an edge. A sequence of vertices (u0, u1, u2, . . . , un) is referred to as a (u0, un)-path
in a graph G if uiui+1 ∈E(G), ∀i ∈{0, 1, . . . , (n −1)}. A cycle (or circuit) in a graph G is a
(u0, un)-path together with an edge u0un. If the graph G itself is a cycle, it is denoted by Cn.
A graph G is triangle-free if there does not exist any cycle of length 3 in G.
The length of the shortest path (geodesic) between two vertices u and v is denoted by dG(u, v).
The maximum distance between any pair of vertices of G is called the diameter of G, denoted
by diam(G).
Let X be any set. The cardinality of X is denoted as |X|. If two sets X1 and X2 are disjoint,
then their union is denoted as X1
U X2. The set of vertices adjacent to a vertex w is called the
neighbor set of w, denoted by N(w), and the degree of a vertex w is given by d(w) = |N(w)|.
The maximum degree of a vertex in a graph G is denoted by ∆(G) or simply, ∆. If a vertex
u ∈N(w1) ∩N(w2), then u is called a common neighbor of w1 and w2. A graph G is said to be
d−regular if every vertex of G has degree exactly d. A d−regular graph G on n vertices is said
to be strongly regular of type (n, d, λ, µ) if every adjacent pair of vertices in G has λ common
neighbors and every non-adjacent pair of vertices has µ common neighbors.
For a given graph G with maximum degree d and diameter k, the maximum possible number
of vertices is n = 1 + d Pk−1
i=0 (d −1)i, which is called the Moore bound. A Moore graph is a
d-regular graph of diameter k which attains the Moore bound. In [18], Hoffman and Singleton
identified Moore graphs of diameter 2. For degree d = 2, the unique Moore graph is the cycle
C5; for d = 3, the Petersen graph and for d = 7, is the Hoffman–Singleton graph. A possible
fourth case, for degree d = 57 would have 3250 vertices; however, its existence remains an open
problem in algebraic graph theory.
A matching in a graph G(V, E) is a set of edges M ⊆E such that no two edges in M share a
common endpoint. An induced matching[8] in G is a matching M in G such that no two edges
of M are joined by an edge of G. If an induced matching consists of exactly k edges, then it


--- Page 3 ---
ON THE MUTUAL VISIBILITY OF SOME MOORE GRAPHS
3
is referred to as an induced k−matching. A subset S ⊆V is said to be an independent set of
vertices of G if no two vertices in S are adjacent in G. Let S, T ⊂V . Then e(S) denotes the
number of edges of G[S] and e(S, T) denotes the number of edges of G having one end in S and
the other end in T. The line graph L(G) of a graph G(V, E) is the graph with vertex set E and
two vertices of L(G) are adjacent if and only if the corresponding edges of G are incident to a
common vertex in G.
A function f : I ⊆R →R defined on an interval I is called convex if, for all x, y ∈I and all
λ ∈[0, 1], we have f(λx + (1 −λ)y) ≤λf(x) + (1 −λ)f(y). Geometrically, this means that the
line segment connecting any two points on the graph of f lies above or on the graph. A function
f : I ⊆R →R defined on an interval I is called concave if, for all x, y ∈I and all λ ∈[0, 1], we
have f(λx + (1 −λ)y) ≥λf(x) + (1 −λ)f(y).
Jensen’s inequality (see [15]): Let f : I →R be a convex function on an interval I ⊆R.
For any integer m ≥1 and any points x1, x2, . . . , xm ∈I and nonnegative weights α1, α2, . . . , αm
with Pm
i=1 αi = 1, we have
(2.1)
f
 m
X
i=1
αixi
!
≤
m
X
i=1
αif(xi)
In particular, for equal weights αi = 1
m,
f

1
m
m
X
i=1
xi

≤1
m
m
X
i=1
f(xi).
If strict inequality holds in (3.2), then we say that f is strictly convex.
Equality condition(see [15]): If f is strictly convex and m ≥2, then equality holds in
Jensen’s inequality if and only if x1 = x2 = · · · = xm.
3. Mutual-visibility sets of some Moore graphs of diameter two
In this section, we characterize the mutual-visibility sets of Moore graphs of diameter two,
focusing in particular on the Petersen graph and the Hoffman–Singleton graph.
Lemma 1. Let G be a graph of diameter 2. A subset S ⊆V (G) is a mutual-visibility set of G if
and only if, for every pair of non-adjacent vertices {u, v} ⊆S, there exists a common neighbour
w ∈V (G) \ S.
Proof. Let S ⊆V (G). If u, v ∈S are nonadjacent, then dG(u, v) = 2. Thus, every shortest (u, v)-
path has exactly one internal vertex, which is a common neighbor of u and v. Consequently, u
and v are S-visible if and only if at least one of their common neighbors lies in V (G) \ S. Hence
S is a mutual-visibility set of G if and only if for every nonadjacent pair {u, v} ⊆S there exists
a common neighbor w /∈S.
□
Lemma 2. Let G be a graph of diameter 2, and let S ⊆V (G) be a mutual-visibility set of size
s. Then:
(i)
s
2

−e(S) ≤
X
w∈V (G)\S
|N(w) ∩S|
2

, where e(S) denotes the number of edges in the
induced subgraph G[S].
(ii) Moreover, if G is triangle-free and every non-adjacent pair {u, v} ⊆S has a unique
common neighbour in V (G) \ S, then equality holds.
Proof. Let U =

{u, v} ⊆S : uv /∈E(G)
	
be the set of unordered non-adjacent pairs of vertices
in S. Then |U| =
␀s
2

−e(S).
By Lemma 1, every non-adjacent pair {u, v} ⊆S has at least one common neighbour w ∈
V (G) \ S. That is, for each {u, v} ∈U there exists at least one vertex w ∈V (G) \ S such that
u, v ∈N(w). For each w ∈V (G) \ S, define F(w) =

{u, v} ⊆S : u, v ∈N(w)
	
.


--- Page 4 ---
4
TONNY K B AND SHIKHI M
(i) By Lemma 1, for every {u, v} ∈U, there exists at least one w ∈V (G) \ S such that
{u, v} ∈F(w) and hence, |F(w)| =
␀|N(w)∩S|
2

. Therefore
|U| ≤
X
w∈V (G)\S
|F(w)| =
X
w∈V (G)\S
|N(w) ∩S|
2

.
Substituting |U| =
␀s
2

−e(S) yields the claimed inequality.
(ii) Now, let G be triangle-free and every non-adjacent pair {u, v} ⊆S has a unique common
neighbour in V (G) \ S. For w ∈V (G) \ S, any two neighbours of w are non-adjacent, since G
is triangle-free. Therefore, F(w) ⊆U.
Let {u, v} ∈U. Since G has diameter 2 and uv /∈E(G), dG(u, v) = 2. Since u and v are
S-visible, there is a shortest (u, v)-path, whose internal vertex lies outside S. Because every
non-adjacent pair has a unique common neighbour, that geodesic is unique and has the form
(u, w, v) with the unique vertex w /∈S. Hence, there exists a unique vertex w ∈V (G) \ S
such that {u, v} ∈F(w).
Therefore the families {F(w)}w/∈S are pairwise disjoint and U =
U
w∈V (G)\S F(w). Taking cardinalities on both sides
s
2

−e(S) =
X
w∈V (G)\S
|F(w)| =
X
w∈V (G)\S
|N(w) ∩S|
2

,
which is the desired equality.
□
Lemma 3. Let G be a graph in which every two non-adjacent vertices have at least one common
neighbour. Then diam(G) is one or two. In particular, if G is not complete, then diam(G) = 2.
Proof. If all the vertices are adjacent, then G is complete and diam(G) = 1. Suppose G is not
complete, then there exist two non-adjacent vertices u, v ∈V (G). By hypothesis, there exists a
unique vertex w adjacent to both u and v. Hence (u, w, v) is a path of length 2, so dG(u, v) = 2.
Therefore, every pair of distinct vertices has distance at most 2, hence diam(G) = 2.
□
Proposition 4. Let G be a graph of diameter 2 on n vertices with maximum degree ∆, where
∆, n ∈Z≥2. If S ⊆V (G) is a mutual-visibility set of size s, then
s2 + (∆2 −2∆−1)s −∆(∆−1)n ≤0,
and consequently
0 ≤s ≤
$
−(∆2 −2∆−1) +
p
(∆2 −2∆−1)2 + 4∆(∆−1)n
2
%
.
Proof. Since 0 ≤|N(w) ∩S| ≤dG(w) ≤∆, we have
|N(w) ∩S|
2

≤
∆
2

for every w /∈S.
Summing over the n −s vertices outside S yields
X
w/∈S
|N(w) ∩S|
2

≤
X
w/∈S

.
By Lemma 2 and the inequality above,

+ e(S).
By the handshaking lemma, 2e(S) = P
v∈S dG[S](v) ≤P
v∈S dG(v) ≤s∆, so that e(S) ≤s∆/2.
Therefore,

--- Page 5 ---
ON THE MUTUAL VISIBILITY OF SOME MOORE GRAPHS
5
Since the quadratic f(s) = s2 + (∆2 −2∆−1)s −∆(∆−1)n has positive leading coefficient,
the inequality f(s) ≤0 holds precisely on the closed interval [r−, r+], where r−and r+ are the
two real roots of f(s). These roots exist because the discriminant of f(s) is
D = (∆2 −2∆−1)2 + 4∆(∆−1)n,
and both terms, (∆2 −2∆−1)2 and 4∆(∆−1)n, are strictly positive for all ∆, n ∈Z≥2. Indeed,
the polynomial ∆2−2∆−1 has roots 1±
√
2, which are not integers, ensuring that (∆2−2∆−1)2 >
0 for all integer ∆. Thus r± = −(∆2−2∆−1)±
√
D
2
. Moreover, f(0) = −∆(∆−1)n < 0, since
∆(∆−1)n > 0 for ∆, n ∈Z≥2. Therefore r−< 0 < r+. As s ≥0 and s is an integer, it follows
that
0 ≤s ≤⌊r+⌋=
$
−(∆2 −2∆−1) +
p
(∆2 −2∆−1)2 + 4∆(∆−1)n
2
%
□
Lemma 5. Let G be a triangle-free graph in which every pair of non-adjacent vertices has a
unique common neighbour. Then a subset S ⊆V (G) is a mutual-visibility set of G if and only
if the induced subgraph G[S] has maximum degree at most 1.
Proof. Since G is triangle-free it cannot be complete; hence, by Lemma 3, we have diam(G) = 2.
(⇒) Suppose S is a mutual-visibility set. Assume that there exists a vertex x ∈S with dG[S](x) ≥
2. Then x has at least two neighbours u, v ∈S, so that (u, x, v) is a path of length two in G.
Since G is triangle-free, u and v are non-adjacent in G. By hypothesis, every non-adjacent pair
has a unique common neighbour, and here x is such a neighbour of u and v. However, because
diam(G) = 2, Lemma 1 implies that u and v must also have a common neighbour in V (G) \ S.
This contradicts the uniqueness of the common neighbour x. Thus, every vertex of G[S] has
degree at most 1.
(⇐) Conversely, suppose that G[S] has maximum degree at most 1, and let u, v ∈S. If u and
v are adjacent, then trivially they are S−visible. If u and v are non-adjacent, let w be their
unique common neighbour. By Lemma 1, we must have w /∈S. Thus (u, w, v) is a shortest
(u, v)-path whose internal vertex lies outside S. Hence S is a mutual-visibility set of G.
□
Corollary 6. Under the hypotheses of Lemma 5, if S ⊆V (G) is a mutual-visibility set, then
0 ≤2e(S) ≤|S|, where e(S) denotes the number of edges in the induced subgraph G[S].
Proof. Clearly, e(S) ≥0. By Lemma 5, G[S] has maximum degree at most 1. Hence it follows
that P
v∈S dG[S](v) ≤|S|.
By the handshaking lemma, P
v∈S dG[S](v) = 2e(S), so 2e(S) ≤
|S|.
□
Lemma 7. Under the hypotheses of Lemma 5, the following conditions are equivalent:
(i) S is a mutual-visibility set of G.
(ii) The induced subgraph G[S] satisfies ∆(G[S]) ≤1.
(iii) G[S] is a disjoint union of an induced matching and isolated vertices.
Proof. (i) ⇐⇒(ii) follows directly from Lemma 5.
(ii) ⇒(iii) Assume ∆(G[S]) ≤1.
Then every connected component of G[S] has maximum
degree at most 1, and hence each component is either K1 or K2. Let M be the set of edges
corresponding to the K2-components. These edges are pairwise disjoint, so M is a matching.
To see that M is induced, let e(M) denote the set of end vertices of edges in M. If there were
an edge in G joining vertices of e(M) belonging to different K2-components, then in G[S] one of
those endpoints would have degree at least 2, contradicting ∆(G[S]) ≤1. Thus G[S] contains
exactly the edges of M, so G[S] is a disjoint union of an induced matching and isolated vertices.
(iii)⇒(ii) If G[S] is a disjoint union of an induced matching and isolated vertices, then every
vertex of G[S] is incident to at most one edge, and hence ∆(G[S]) ≤1.
This completes the proof of equivalence.
□


--- Page 6 ---
6
TONNY K B AND SHIKHI M
Lemma 8. Let G be a triangle-free, d-regular graph where d ≥3, in which every pair of non-
adjacent vertices has a unique common neighbour. If S ⊆V (G) is a mutual-visibility set of size
s, then s2 + 4s −d(d −1)n ≤0, and consequently
0 ≤s ≤
j
−2 +
p
4 + d(d −1)n
k
.
Proof. Since G is triangle-free it cannot be complete. Hence, by Lemma 3, we have diam(G) = 2.
Let S ⊆V (G) be a mutual-visibility set with |S| = s, then by Lemma 2 (ii),
s
2

−e(S) =
X
w∈V (G)\S
|N(w) ∩S|
2

.
Since 0 ≤|N(w) ∩S| ≤dG(w) = d, we have
|N(w) ∩S|
2

≤
d
2

for every w /∈S.
Summing over the n −s vertices outside S yields
X
w/∈S
|N(w) ∩S|
2

≤
X
w/∈S
d
2

= (n −s)
d
2

.
Therefore,
(3.1)
s
2

−e(S) ≤(n −s)
d
2

Multiplying by 2 and rearranging gives
(3.2)
s2 +
␀d(d −1) −1

s −2e(S) −d(d −1) n ≤0.
By Corollary 6, 0 ≤2e(S) ≤s, so from (3.2) we obtain s2 +
␀d(d −1) −2

s −d(d −1) n ≤0.
For d ≥3, we have d(d −1) −2 ≥4 and hence s2 + 4s −d(d −1) n ≤0. Consider the function
f(s) = s2+4s−d(d−1)n. Since the leading coefficient of f(s) is positive, the inequality f(s) ≤0
holds precisely for those values of s lying between its two real roots r−and r+. These roots
exist because the discriminant of f(s) is D = 42 +4d(d−1)n = 16+4d(d−1)n > 0, since d ≥3.
Hence,
r± = −4 ±
√
D
2
= −4 ±
p
16 + 4d(d −1)n
2
= −2 ±
p
4 + d(d −1)n .
Therefore, the inequality f(s) ≤0 holds for all
s ∈[r−, r+] =

−2 −
p
4 + d(d −1)n , −2 +
p
4 + d(d −1)n

.
Since s represents the cardinality of a subset of vertices, it must be a non-negative integer. Note
that f(0) = −d(d −1)n is negative, as d ≥3. Hence r−< 0 < r+, and it follows that
0 ≤s ≤
j
−2 +
p
4 + d(d −1)n
k
.
□
Definition 9. The Petersen graph P has one vertex for each 2-element subset of {1, 2, 3, 4, 5},
and two vertices are adjacent if and only if the corresponding subsets are disjoint. Equivalently,
(i) P is isomorphic to the complement of the line graph of K5.
(ii) P is the unique 3-regular graph on 10 vertices with girth 5 and diameter 2.
Lemma 10 ([26, Cor. 6(ii)]). The number of induced k-matchings in the Petersen graph is five
if k = 3 and fifteen if k = 2.
Theorem 11. Let P denote the Petersen graph. Then µ(P) = 6 and rµ(P) = 5.


--- Page 7 ---
ON THE MUTUAL VISIBILITY OF SOME MOORE GRAPHS
7
Proof. Let S be a mutual-visibility set of P of size s. Since P is triangle-free, 3-regular, and
every pair of nonadjacent vertices has a unique common neighbour, Lemma 8 implies that
s2 + 4s −60 ≤0. The roots of s2 + 4s −60 are −10 and 6, so the inequality holds whenever
−10 ≤s ≤6. Substituting n = 10 and d = 3 into (3.1) yields
(3.3)
s
2

−e(S) ≤3(10 −s).
By Corollary 6, we also have 2e(S) ≤s. For s = 6, this gives e(S) ≤3, while the inequality
(3.3) implies e(S) ≥3. Hence e(S) = 3. By Lemma 7(iii), it follows that P[S] is an induced
matching of size 3 (with no isolated vertices). Conversely, Lemma 7 shows that every induced
matching of size 3 corresponds to a mutual-visibility set of size 6.
Finally, by Lemma 10, the Petersen graph contains exactly five induced 3-matchings. There-
fore, µ(P) = 6 and rµ(P) = 5.
□
Theorem 12 ([9, Th. 18]). If G has no triangles and has order 10, then G has at least five
independent sets of 4 vertices; there are exactly five such sets if G is one of three graphs, one of
which is the Petersen graph.
Lemma 13. Let P be the Petersen graph and let uv ∈E(P). Then X = V (P) \
␀N(u) ∪N(v)

has size 4, and the induced subgraph P[X] is isomorphic to 2K2.
Proof. Let uv ∈E(P). Since adjacent vertices of P have no common neighbors and since P is
3-regular, we have |N(u) ∪N(v)| = |N(u)| + |N(v)| = 3 + 3 = 6, and hence |X| = 10 −6 = 4.
Note that P ∼= L(K5). Therefore, the vertices u and v are non-adjacent in L(K5). If ab and cd
are the edges of K5 corresponding to u and v, then ab and cd are non-adjacent.
The set X consists precisely of the vertices that are non-adjacent to both u and v. A vertex
x of P is non-adjacent to both u and v if and only if x is adjacent to both u and v in L(K5);
equivalently, the edge in K5 corresponding to x must meet both ab and cd. There are exactly
four such edges in K5, namely ac, ad, bc, and bd. Thus X corresponds to these four edges,
which form a 4-cycle (a, c, b, d) in K5. Hence the vertices of L(K5) corresponding to the edges
ac, ad, bc, bd induce a 4-cycle in L(K5). Therefore, in P = L(K5) the induced subgraph on X is
the complement of C4, namely 2K2.
□
Theorem 14. V(P) = 1 + 10x + 45x2 + 90x3 + 80x4 + 30x5 + 5x6.
Proof. The first three terms of the polynomial are straightforward: the constant term 1 cor-
responds to the empty set, the coefficient 10 counts the singleton subsets of V (P), and the
coefficient 45 arises from the
␀10
2

pairs of vertices, each of which forms a mutual-visibility set.
By Lemma 7(iii), a subset S ⊆V (P) is a mutual-visibility set of P if and only if the induced
subgraph P[S] is a disjoint union of induced matchings and isolated vertices. We further use
the facts that P is triangle-free, 3-regular and P ∼= L(K5). Let k be the size of mutual-visibility
set of P.
Case k = 3: The possible forms of P[S] are 3K1 and K2 ∪K1; the only forbidden type is
P3 (which is not a matching). There are
␀10
3

= 120 triples of vertices in total. An induced
subgraph P3 is determined uniquely by its center (10 choices) together with two of its three
neighbors (
␀3
2

= 3 choices), giving 10 × 3 = 30 such triples. Therefore, r3 = 120 −30 = 90.
Case k = 4: The possible induced subgraphs P[S] are 4K1, 2K2, and K2 ∪2K1.
• If P[S] ∼= 4K1, then S is an independent set of four vertices. By Theorem 12, there are
exactly 5 such independent sets in P.
• If P[S] ∼= 2K2, then P[S] an induced 2-matching. By Lemma 10, the number of induced
2-matchings in P is 15.
• Suppose P[S] ∼= K2 ∪2K1. Let uv be an edge of P (15 choices). By Lemma 13, the set
X = V (P) \
␀N(u) ∪N(v)

consists precisely of the vertices that are non-adjacent to both u
and v, so |X| = 4, and the induced subgraph P[X] is isomorphic to 2K2. Hence, among the
four vertices in X, exactly
␀4
2

−2 = 4 pairs are non-adjacent. The edge uv together with such


--- Page 8 ---
8
TONNY K B AND SHIKHI M
a non-adjacent pair yields an induced subgraph K2 ∪2K1. Therefore, there are 15 × 4 = 60
subsets of this type. Altogether, we obtain r4 = 5 + 15 + 60 = 80.
Case k = 5: The only possible types for a 5-vertex induced subgraph P[S] are 5K1, K2 ∪
3K1, 2K2 ∪K1.
• The case 5K1 cannot occur, since α(P) = 4.
• The case K2 ∪3K1 also cannot occur, because, for any edge uv(K2), the four vertices
non-adjacent to both u and v induce 2K2 by Lemma 13. So one cannot pick three isolated
vertices.
• The only possible structure is 2K2 ∪K1. By Lemma 10, the Petersen graph has exactly 15
induced 2-matchings (2K2). For each such 2K2, under the identification P ∼= L(K5), the four
vertices correspond to a 4-cycle in L(K5). A vertex x of P is non-adjacent to all four vertices of
this 2K2 if and only if, in L(K5), the vertex x is adjacent to all four vertices of the corresponding
C4; equivalently, the edge of K5 corresponding to x is one of the two diagonals of the underlying
4-cycle in K5. Thus, there are exactly two choices for x. Hence each induced 2K2 extends in
exactly two ways to 2K2 ∪K1, and therefore r5 = 15 × 2 = 30. This completes the proof.
□
Lemma 15. Let G be a graph, let S ⊆V (G), and put T = V (G) \ S. Then
e(S, T) =
X
v∈S
degG(v) −2e(S) =
X
v∈S
␀degG(v) −degG[S](v)

.
where e(S) denotes the number of edges in the induced subgraph G[S]. In particular, if G is
d-regular, then e(S, T) = d|S| −2e(S).
Proof. For each vertex v ∈S let t(v) be the number of edges from v to T = V (G) \ S. Clearly
for every v ∈S,
degG(v) = degG[S](v) + t(v).
Summing this identity over all v ∈S yields
X
v∈S
degG(v) =
X
v∈S
degG[S](v) +
X
v∈S
t(v).
By the Handshaking Lemma applied to the induced subgraph G[S], we have P
v∈S degG[S](v) =
2e(S). Moreover, the sum P
v∈S t(v) counts each edge with one endpoint in S and one in T
exactly once, so that
X
v∈S
t(v) = e(S, T).
Therefore,
X
v∈S
degG(v) = 2e(S) + e(S, T),
and rearranging yields
e(S, T) =
X
v∈S
degG(v) −2e(S) =
X
v∈S
␀degG(v) −degG[S](v)

.
If G is d-regular then P
v∈S degG(v) = d|S|, whence e(S, T) = d|S| −2e(S).
□
Definition 16. The Hoffman–Singleton graph is the unique graph H having the following equiv-
alent descriptions:
(i) H is the unique strongly regular graph with parameters srg(50, 7, 0, 1).
(ii) H is the Moore graph of degree 7 and diameter 2.
The following remark is straightforward from the definition of the Hoffman-Singleton graph.
Remark 17. The Hoffman–Singleton graph H possesses the following properties.
(i) H is triangle-free.
(ii) H contains no 4-cycles. Hence, the girth of H is 5.
Proposition 18. Let H be the Hoffman–Singleton graph. If S ⊆V (H) is a mutual-visibility
set of size s, then µ(H) ≤26.


--- Page 9 ---
ON THE MUTUAL VISIBILITY OF SOME MOORE GRAPHS
9
Proof. Let S ⊆V (H) be a mutual-visibility set and put s = |S| and T = V (H)\S. By Lemma 15
we have
e(S, T) =
X
v∈S
degH(v) −2e(S) = ds −2e(S),
where d = 7 and n = 50 for the Hoffman–Singleton graph. Since each vertex of T has at most
d neighbours in S, we obtain
e(S, T) ≤d|T| = d(n −s).
Combining the inequality with last equation we get, ds −2e(S) ≤d(n −s). Hence,
ds ≤dn
2 + e(S).
By Corollary 6, we have, e(S) ≤s/2. Hence,
ds ≤dn
2 + s
2,
and therefore (2d −1)s ≤dn. Thus
s ≤
dn
2d −1,
and for d = 7, n = 50 we obtain
s ≤
7 · 50
13

= 26.
Hence µ(H) ≤26, as claimed.
□
In Proposition 18, we establish an upper bound of 26 for the size of any mutual-visibility set in
the Hoffman–Singleton graph. Although this provides a useful restriction on the possible size of
such sets, the bound is not tight. In the subsequent analysis, we refine our approach and derive
a sharper upper bound, showing that the size of a mutual-visibility set in Hoffman-Singleton
graph cannot exceed 20.
Theorem 19 ([15, Th. 7.1] ). Let f : (a, b) →R and suppose that f′′(x) exists for every
x ∈(a, b). Then f is convex on (a, b) if and only if f′′(x) ≥0
for all x ∈(a, b). Moreover, if
f′′(x) > 0 for all x ∈(a, b), then f is strictly convex on (a, b).
Theorem 20. Let H be the Hoffman–Singleton graph. Then µ(H) ≤20. Moreover, if a mutual-
visibility set S ⊆V (H) of size 20 exists, then the induced subgraph H[S] is an induced matching
consisting of ten disjoint edges and every vertex of T = V (H) \ S has exactly four neighbours in
S.
Proof. Let S ⊆V (H) be a mutual-visibility set of size s, put T = V (H) \ S and write e(S) for
the number of edges in H[S].
For each t ∈T, let kt = |N(t) ∩S|. Then by Lemma 2 (ii),
(3.4)
s
2

−e(S) =
X
t∈T
kt
2

.
By definition, let kt denote the number of edges joining a vertex t ∈T to vertices of S. Hence
P
t∈T kt is the total number of edges with one endpoint in T and the other in S; that is,
X
t∈T
kt = e(S, T).
Therefore, by Lemma 15, we obtain
(3.5)
X
t∈T
kt = e(S, T) = 7s −2e(S)
Now let f(x) =
␀x
2

for x ∈R. Since f′′(x) = 1 > 0, the function f is strictly convex on R.
Applying Jensen’s inequality to f with the nonnegative numbers kt ≥0 (for t ∈T) and equal
weights 1/|T|, we obtain


--- Page 10 ---
10
TONNY K B AND SHIKHI M
f
 
1
|T|
X
t∈T
kt
!
≤1
|T|
X
t∈T
f(kt)
f
 1
|T|(7s −2e(S))

≤1
|T|
X
t∈T
kt
2

X
t∈T
kt
2

≥|T|
 7s−2e(S)
|T|
2

= (7s −2e(S))2
2|T|
−7s −2e(S)
2
Using the equation (3.4), we get
(3.6)
s
2

−e(S) ≥(7s −2e(S))2
2(50 −s)
−7s −2e(S)
2
Fix s and, for brevity, write e = e(S). Define
F(e) = (7s −2e)2
2(50 −s) −7s −2e
2
,
D(e) =
s
2

−e −F(e).
The value of e associated to a mutual-visibility set S must satisfy D(e) ≥0. Since H is
triangle-free and every pair of non-adjacent vertices has a unique common neighbour, by Corol-
lary 6, e(S) ≤s/2. Hence, for a fixed s, the existence of any admissible e ∈[0, s/2] is equivalent
to the condition
max
0≤e≤s/2 D(e) ≥0.
Indeed, if the maximum of D on [0, s/2] were negative, then D(e) < 0 for every feasible e and
no set S of size s could satisfy the inequality (3.6).
Thus, to obtain a necessary condition on s, we examine the maximum value of D(e) over the
closed interval [0, s/2]. A straightforward calculus check shows that
F ′′(e) =
4
50 −s.
By Proposition 18, we have s ≤26, and hence F ′′(e) > 0. Therefore, F is convex, and con-
sequently, −F is concave. Since,
␀s
2

−e is a linear function in e, it is concave. Therefore,
D(e) =
␀s
2

−e−F(e) is concave on [0, s/2]. Consequently, max0≤e≤s/2 D(e) is attained either at
a stationary point or at one of the endpoints. Computing D′(e) yields the unique critical point
e0 = 4s −25, and
4s −25 > s
2 ⇐⇒s ≥8
Hence if s ≥8, then the stationary point lies to the right of the feasible interval and the concave
function D is increasing on [0, s/2]; therefore the maximum is attained at the right endpoint
e = s/2. Accordingly a necessary condition for existence of a feasible e is D
␀s/2

≥0, that is,
s
2

−s
2 ≥
␀7s −2 · s
2
2
2(50 −s)
−7s −2 · s
2
2
s(s −2)
2
≥
(6s)2
2(50 −s) −6s
2
s −2 ≥
36s
50 −s −6
(50 −s)(s + 4) ≥36s
⇐⇒
s2 −10s −200 ≤0
Hence s ≤20. Therefore µ(H) ≤20.
If s = 20, then equality holds throughout the above chain. In particular, e(S) = s/2 = 10,
and equality in Jensen’s inequality implies that kt is constant for all t ∈T, since f is strictly


--- Page 11 ---
ON THE MUTUAL VISIBILITY OF SOME MOORE GRAPHS
11
convex. From Equation (3.5), we have
X
t∈T
kt = 7s −2e(S)
=⇒
|T| kt = 7s −2e(S)
=⇒
kt = 7s −2e(S)
|T|
= 7 · 20 −20
30
= 4,
for every t ∈T.
Hence, by Lemma 5, H[S] consists of exactly ten disjoint edges, that is, an induced matching,
and since kt = 4, each vertex t ∈T has exactly four neighbours in S.
□
Next, we verify that the upper bound s ≤20 is sharp. To this end, we formulate the following
integer program.
Integer-program formulation. Under the hypothesis of Lemma 5, a subset S ⊆V (G) is a
mutual-visibility set if and only if the induced subgraph G[S] has maximum degree at most 1.
This combinatorial condition can be captured by the following set of zero-one constraints.
Let S be a mutual-visibility set of G. Introduce binary variables xv ∈{0, 1} for each v ∈V (G),
where xv = 1 indicates that v ∈S, and xv = 0 otherwise.
If v ∈S, then by Lemma 5,
degG[S](v) ≤1; that is, at most one vertex u ∈NG(v) belongs to S. Hence,
X
u∈NG(v)
xu ≤1.
If v /∈S, then Lemma 5 imposes no restriction on P
u∈NG(v) xu, which in this case equals degG(v).
Combining these two cases yields
X
u∈NG(v)
xu ≤1 + degG(v) (1 −xv),
for every v ∈V (G).
Thus, the feasible 0–1 vectors x correspond precisely to those subsets S for which ∆(G[S]) ≤1,
and therefore, by Lemma 7, to the mutual-visibility sets of G.
Therefore the mutual-visibility number µ(G) is given by the following zero-one integer pro-
gram:
(IP–MV)

















maximize
X
v∈V (G)
xv
subject to
X
u∈NG(v)
xu ≤1 + degG(v) (1 −xv)
for all v ∈V (G),
xv ∈{0, 1}
for all v ∈V (G)
For the Hoffman–Singleton graph, the constraints of the integer program (IP-MV) becomes
X
u∈NG(v)
xu ≤1 + 7(1 −xv) ⇐⇒
X
u∈NG(v)
xu + 7xv ≤8
for all v ∈V (H).
The integer linear programming implementation in Python yields an optimal value of (IP–MV)
equal to 20 for the Hoffman–Singleton graph, with a corresponding mutual-visibility set
S = {0, 2, 12, 17, 19, 21, 24, 26, 31, 32, 33, 35, 37, 39, 40, 43, 44, 45, 48, 49}
of size 20. The adjacency matrix of the Hoffman–Singleton graph was taken from the function
hoffman singleton graph() in the networkx package.
Corollary 21. The maximum size of an induced matching in the Hoffman–Singleton graph is
ten.
Proof. Note that the Hoffman–Singleton graph H satisfies the hypothesis of Lemma 5. Therefore,
by Lemma 7, a vertex set S is a mutual-visibility set of H if and only if the induced subgraph
H[S] is a disjoint union of an induced matching and isolated vertices. Hence, for every induced
matching in H, the set of its vertices forms a mutual-visibility set. Consequently, the cardinality
of the vertex set of any induced matching is at most the maximum cardinality of a mutual-
visibility set. Since µ(H) ≤20, no induced matching in H can contain more than 20 vertices.


--- Page 12 ---
12
TONNY K B AND SHIKHI M
By the integer programming formulation, we established that the maximum size of a mutual-
visibility set in the Hoffman–Singleton graph is 20.
Furthermore, by Theorem 20, if S is a
mutual-visibility set of the Hoffman–Singleton graph of size 20, then the induced subgraph H[S]
is a disjoint union of ten edges; that is, H[S] forms an induced matching of size 10. Hence, the
maximum size of an induced matching in the Hoffman–Singleton graph is 10.
□
4. Conclusion
In this paper, we investigated the visibility properties of certain Moore graphs, which serve
as extremal examples in graph theory owing to their exceptional regularity and symmetry. We
began by explicitly deriving the visibility polynomial of the Petersen graph.
For the larger
and more intricate Hoffman–Singleton graph, we first established an upper bound of 20 for
its visibility number. Using integer programming techniques, we then demonstrated that this
bound is tight, proving that the visibility number of the Hoffman–Singleton graph is exactly
20. As a corollary, we deduced that the maximum size of an induced matching in this graph
is 10. These findings illustrate how algebraic computation and optimization methods can be
effectively integrated in the analysis of visibility polynomials and related graph parameters,
suggesting the potential applicability of this framework to other families of highly symmetric or
extremal graphs.
Declarations
Funding sources. This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in
the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
Conflict of interest. The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.
Data Availability. No data was used for the research described in the article
